revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. [[Category:Nagano Festivals]] [[Category:Buddhist Rituals]] [[Category:Jishū]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Saku, Nagano]] [[Category:Fūryū-odori]] [[ja:跡部の踊り念仏]] ''[[ja:和合の念仏踊]] [[ja:新野の盆踊り]] [[ja:郡上踊]] [[ja:寒水の掛踊]] [[ja:徳山の盆踊]] [[ja:有東木の盆踊]] [[ja:綾渡の夜念仏と盆踊]]''
''[[ja:勝手神社の神事踊]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:Atobe Dancing Nembutsu]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:Atobe Dancing Nembutsu]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==

'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]

[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]

* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.

== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. ==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]

[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.

Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]

[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. ==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] [[Category:nowiki former closing]]
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=[[Category:nowiki former closing]]https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. [[Category:Nagano Festivals]] [[Category:Buddhist Rituals]] [[Category:Jishū]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Saku, Nagano]] [[Category:Fūryū-odori]] [[ja:跡部の踊り念仏]] ''[[ja:和合の念仏踊]] [[ja:新野の盆踊り]] [[ja:郡上踊]] [[ja:寒水の掛踊]] [[ja:徳山の盆踊]] [[ja:有東木の盆踊]] [[ja:綾渡の夜念仏と盆踊]]''

==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Tomono Shō (伴野荘) == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] '''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == Overview == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == History == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == References == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] == See Also == [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]][[Category:11]] * ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.

==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
'''[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]]'''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
''

==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
