The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft|12=draft}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

revision.comment: 29 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡) up to rev 95742337
revision.text:
'''伴野荘'''（とものしょう）は、[[信濃国]][[佐久郡]]（現在の[[長野県]][[佐久市]]伴野）にあった[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]。
[[千曲川]]左岸の野沢平、後に[[南佐久郡]]と称された地域の大部分を占める荘園であるが、[[大井荘 (信濃国)|大井荘]]・平賀郷との境界が錯綜しているため<ref> 「長野県史 通史編 第2巻」p.284</ref>、正確な荘域は不明。[[諏訪大社]]の神役注文などの史料から、伴野、野沢、臼田、海野口、大石、鷹野、小宮山、下県、桜井、三塚、大日向などの諸郷村が含まれていたことが判明している<ref> 「長野県史 通史編 第2巻」p.285</ref>。同じ信濃国に[[伴野荘 (信濃国伊那郡)|伴野荘]]（[[伊那郡]]）が存在するため、「'''佐久伴野荘'''」とも表記される。
== 歴史 == 『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]2年[[3月12日 (旧暦)|3月12日]]（[[1186年]][[4月3日]]）条に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]から[[源頼朝]]に示された「関東御知行国々内乃具未済庄々注文」にその名が見られる。当時の[[領家]]は[[持明院基家|藤原基家]]、[[地頭]]は[[小笠原長清]]であった。同4年[[9月22日 (旧暦)|9月22日]]（[[1188年]][[11月3日]]）条では、頼朝が長清に滞納する年貢をおさめるように命じたことを[[吉田経房|藤原経房]]に進上した。その後[[本家]]は後白河法皇の孫である[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]、領家は基家の娘で[[守貞親王]]（後の後高倉院）の妃であった[[藤原陳子|陳子]]（後の北白川院）に継承された。ところが、[[承久の乱]]の後に[[鎌倉幕府]]によって後鳥羽上皇の荘園が守貞親王のものとなり、親王と陳子の娘である[[利子内親王]]（式乾門院）は父から本家職、母から領家職を継ぐことになる。その後、利子内親王の姪である[[暉子内親王]]（室町院）がこれを継承した。
一方、地頭職は小笠原長清の六男[[伴野時長|小笠原時長]]が継承して[[伴野氏]]を名乗ったが、[[弘安]]8年（[[1285年]]）の[[霜月騒動]]で縁戚の[[安達氏]]に[[連座]]して[[北条氏]]に地頭職を奪われた。[[嘉暦]]4年（[[1329年]]）の[[諏訪大社]]上社頭役・造営に関する文書からは、大沢・鷹野郷が北条氏、三塚・小宮郷が[[名越流北条氏|名越氏]]、桜井・野沢・臼田郷が[[金沢流北条氏|金沢氏]]、下県郷が[[足利氏]]の所領となっていたことが窺える<ref>「長野県史 通史編 第2巻」p.160</ref>。
霜月騒動の少し前である[[弘安]]2年（[[1279年]]）の冬、[[善光寺]]に向かう途中であった[[一遍]]が、伯父の[[河野氏|河野通末]]の[[流罪|配流]]先であった伴野荘を訪れ、この地の市場において初めて[[踊念仏]]を行ったと『[[一遍聖絵]]』にある。
暉子内親王没後の[[乾元 (日本)|乾元]]元年（[[1302年]]）、その遺領は「室町院領」として再編されて[[伏見天皇|伏見上皇]]のものとなり、以降本家職・領家職を[[持明院統]]が継承した。[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）、持明院統の[[花園天皇|花園上皇]]は領家職を[[大徳寺]]に[[寄進]]、3年後の[[元弘の乱]]で北条氏が滅亡して地頭職が[[大覚寺統]]の[[後醍醐天皇]]によって没収されると、天皇はこれを大徳寺に寄進したため、領家職・地頭職は大徳寺のものとなった。[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1335年]]）10月、伴野荘の[[雑掌]]が大徳寺に[[年貢]][[注文]]を注進しているが、それによると同地では[[代銭納]]が進んでおり毎年銭約7,600文が惣年貢（領家分+地頭分）が納められていた。しかし[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]には、[[足利尊氏]]に従って倒幕に参加した伴野氏が、地頭職回復を求めて押領したため、[[建武 (日本)|建武]]2年（1335年）に大徳寺が[[雑訴決断所]]に訴えたが<ref>「長野県史 通史編 第3巻」p.32</ref>、[[悪党]]の蜂起などによって支配が不安定になり、[[貞和]]5年（[[正平 (日本)|正平]]4年:[[1349年]]）に[[小槻匡遠]]が大徳寺に対して[[伊勢神宮]]への[[役夫工米]]課税の田数を注進したことの記録を最後に姿を消した。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == *井原今朝男「伴野荘」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 10』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1989年）ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 *郷道哲章「伴野荘」『[[日本史大事典]] 5』（[[平凡社]] 1993年）ISBN 978-4-582-13105-5 *井原今朝男「伴野荘」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 3』（[[小学館]] 2001年）ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 *『日本歴史地名大系 20 <small>長野県の地名</small>』（平凡社 1979年）ISBN 978-4-582-49020-6 *『[[長野県史]] 通史編 第2巻 中世1』 *『長野県史 通史編 第3巻 中世2』
== 関連項目 == *[[跡部の踊り念仏]]

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''

[[sn:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]] [[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]] [[ja:跡部の踊り念仏]] [[ja:和合の念仏踊]] [[ja:新野の盆踊り]] [[ja:郡上踊]] [[ja:寒水の掛踊]] [[ja:徳山の盆踊]] [[ja:有東木の盆踊]] [[ja:綾渡の夜念仏と盆踊]] [[ja:勝手神社の神事踊]] [[en:Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]] [[sn:dagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province]] {{translated page|ja|伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)|version=95742337|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=draft}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. == History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.

* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.

==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''

''
''

'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''

The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''
