== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.

== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.

==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17229361]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of [[:en:Ji-shu|Ji-shu]] during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''

== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''

{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.

Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
''
''
'' ''

revision.text:
'''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}'''is a manor in Japan. It is where [[Ippen]] first performed the {{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} dance.
==Overview==
== Tomono Shō (伴野荘) ==
'''Tomono Shō''' was a manor estate (''shōen'') located in Saku District of Shinano Province, present-day Tomono, Saku City, Nagano Prefecture.
== Overview ==
The estate occupied a large part of the region later known as Minami-Saku District, situated on the left bank of the Chikuma River. Due to complex boundaries with neighboring Oi Shō and Hiraga Village, the exact borders of Tomono Shō remain unclear. Historical records, such as administrative documents from Suwa Taisha, indicate that the estate included villages like Tomono, Nozawa, Usuda, Unnoguchi, Ōishi, Takano, Komiyama, Shimogata, Sakurai, Mitsuka, and Ōhinata. To distinguish it from another manor of the same name in Ina District, it was also referred to as "Saku Tomono Shō."
== History ==
The earliest mention of Tomono Shō appears in the ''Azuma Kagami'' on March 12, 1186, in an order from Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa to Minamoto no Yoritomo listing manors under the control of the Kamakura shogunate. At that time, the manor's ''ryōke'' (landholder) was Fujiwara no Motouji, and the ''jitō'' (estate steward) was Ogasawara Nagakiyo.
By 1188, Yoritomo had ordered Nagakiyo to remit overdue taxes, as recorded in correspondence with Fujiwara no Tsuneie. Later, the ''honke'' (head estate) was inherited by Retired Emperor Go-Toba, and the ''ryōke'' position passed to Motouji's daughter, Fujiwara no Chinshi (later known as Hokushirakawa-in), consort of Prince Morisada. After the Jōkyū War (1221), the Kamakura shogunate transferred the estate to Prince Morisada. Their daughter, Imperial Princess Toshiko, inherited both the ''honke'' and ''ryōke'' roles, followed by her niece, Imperial Princess Teruko.
The ''jitō'' position was inherited by Ogasawara Tokinaga, the sixth son of Nagakiyo, who adopted the name Tomono and founded the Tomono clan. However, during the Shimotsuki Disturbance in 1285, the Tomono clan lost their position due to their association with the Adachi clan, and the stewardship was seized by the Hōjō clan.
Documents from 1329 regarding the reconstruction of Suwa Taisha reveal that the estate was fragmented among various clans: Ōsawa and Takano were under the Hōjō, Mitsuka and Komiya under the Nagoe branch of the Hōjō, Sakurai, Nozawa, and Usuda under the Kanazawa branch of the Hōjō, and Shimogata under the Ashikaga clan.
In 1279, the Buddhist monk Ippen visited Tomono Shō, where he performed his first ''odori-nenbutsu'' (dancing chant), as documented in ''Ippen Hijiri-e''.
After Imperial Princess Teruko's death in 1302, the estate was reorganized under Retired Emperor Fushimi. In 1330, Retired Emperor Hanazono donated the ''ryōke'' rights to Daitoku-ji Temple. Following the fall of the Hōjō during the Kenmu Restoration (1333), Emperor Go-Daigo confiscated the estate's stewardship and also donated it to Daitoku-ji.
Records from 1335 show that the manor's administrators submitted tax reports to Daitoku-ji, noting annual payments of approximately 7,600 mon. However, during the Nanboku-chō period, the Tomono clan reclaimed the estate, leading to disputes. The last known record dates to 1349, after which Tomono Shō disappears from historical sources.
== References ==
* Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Kokushi Daijiten'' Vol. 10, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1989. * Gōdo Tetsuaki, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihonshi Daijiten'' Vol. 5, Heibonsha, 1993. * Ibara Kachao, "Tomono Shō," ''Nihon Rekishi Daijiten'' Vol. 3, Shogakukan, 2001. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 20: Nagano-ken no Chimei'', Heibonsha, 1979. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 2: Medieval Period 1''. * ''Nagano Kenshi, Tsuushihen Vol. 3: Medieval Period 2''.
== See Also ==
* ''Atobe no Odori Nembutsu''
==Atobe Dancing Nembutsu==
{{ill|Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|ja|跡部の踊り念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe Dancing Nembutsu|qq=}} (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a '''traditional folk performance''' (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in [[Atobe, Saku, Nagano]], '''Nagano Prefecture'''<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the '''Odori Nenbutsu''' (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of '''Ippen''', the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|WD=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} during the '''Kamakura period''', it has been designated as a '''National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>, and in '''2022''', it was '''inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07]]</ref>.
== Overview== It is said that '''Ippen''' first performed '''Odori Nenbutsu''' in '''{{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|qq=}}''' in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in '''Atobe District'''. Originally, the dance was performed '''outdoors''', but today, it takes place inside the '''main hall of Saifō-ji Temple''' (''西方寺''), where a '''"dōjō" (道場, sacred space)''' is set up. The festival is held annually in '''April''', where '''men and women''' perform by '''beating drums and gongs''' while chanting '''Nenbutsu'''. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across '''the Saku region''', only the '''Saifō-ji version''' has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>.
''''''
== '''The "Purple Cloud" Legend''' == - According to tradition, '''Ippen was inspired to dance''' upon seeing a '''purple cloud'''. - About '''300m south of Atobe''', there is a '''Jishū temple, Kintai-ji''', whose '''mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)'''<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>.
